question.text: Rajesh invested INR 50 lakh in an AIF. He has received distributions of INR 30 lakh. The DPI is:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** DPI = Distributions / Paid-In = 30 / 50 = 0.6x. Rajesh has received back 60% of his investment so far. He needs more distributions to recover his full investment (DPI ≥ 1.0x). **The Trap:** A DPI of 0.6x doesn't mean the investment is bad — the fund may have significant unrealized value (RVPI) that will be distributed later through exits. **Cross-Exam Context:** DPI calculations are tested in XIX-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** A DPI of 0.6x early in a fund's life is normal. Context matters — check the fund's vintage year and where it is in its lifecycle. A 0.6x DPI in year 3 of a 7-year fund is on track; in year 7, it would be concerning.
